Front: 10 marker: Explain two functions of education (Functionalism)
Back: 1. Social solidarity (Durkheim) – education teaches shared values and norms.\n2. Role allocation (Parsons) – education sorts individuals into appropriate jobs based on ability.

Front: 10 marker: Explain two ways education reproduces class inequality (Marxism)
Back: 1. Hidden curriculum – teaches obedience and acceptance of hierarchy.\n2. Correspondence principle – school mirrors workplace, preparing working-class pupils for exploitation.

Front: 10 marker: Explain two external factors affecting achievement
Back: 1. Material deprivation – lack of resources impacts learning.\n2. Cultural deprivation – lack of language and skills needed for school success.

Front: 10 marker: Explain two internal factors affecting achievement
Back: 1. Labelling – teachers’ perceptions influence outcomes.\n2. Streaming – ability grouping can reinforce inequality.

Front: 10 marker: Explain two effects of marketisation
Back: 1. Competition between schools improves standards.\n2. Cream skimming advantages middle-class students.

Front: 10 marker: Explain two gender differences in achievement
Back: 1. Girls outperform boys due to organisation and coursework skills.\n2. Boys may underachieve due to laddish subcultures.

Front: 10 marker: Explain two ethnicity differences in achievement
Back: 1. Teacher labelling can negatively affect some groups.\n2. Institutional racism may disadvantage minority students.

Front: 20 marker: Evaluate the view that education is meritocratic
Back:
AO1: Functionalists (Parsons) argue education is meritocratic.
AO3: Marxists argue meritocracy is a myth.
AO3: Evidence of class inequality persists.
Conclusion: Limited meritocracy due to structural inequality.

Front: 20 marker: Evaluate the Marxist view of education
Back:
AO1: Education reproduces inequality (Bowles & Gintis).
AO3: Supported by correspondence principle.
AO3: Functionalists argue education benefits society.
Conclusion: Marxism highlights inequality but is one-sided.

Front: 20 marker: Evaluate the impact of labelling on achievement
Back:
AO1: Labelling leads to self-fulfilling prophecy.
AO3: Supported by Rosenthal & Jacobson.
AO3: Deterministic and ignores pupil agency.
Conclusion: Important but not sole factor.

Front: 20 marker: Evaluate the role of external factors in achievement
Back:
AO1: Material and cultural deprivation.
AO3: Supported by studies like Douglas.
AO3: Interactionists argue in-school factors also matter.
Conclusion: External factors significant but not exclusive.

Front: 20 marker: Evaluate the role of internal factors in achievement
Back:
AO1: Labelling, streaming and subcultures.
AO3: Supported by Becker and Lacey.
AO3: External factors also influence outcomes.
Conclusion: Internal factors important but interact with external ones.

Front: 20 marker: Evaluate the impact of marketisation policies
Back:
AO1: League tables and competition.
AO3: Raises standards.
AO3: Increases inequality (cream skimming).
Conclusion: Benefits some but disadvantages others.

Front: 20 marker: Evaluate gender differences in education
Back:
AO1: Girls outperform boys.
AO3: Linked to feminism and changing roles.
AO3: Boys affected by masculinity norms.
Conclusion: Gender gap exists but is complex.

AO1: Differences between ethnic groups.
AO3: Labelling and institutional racism.
AO3: Cultural and material factors also matter.
Conclusion: Multiple factors explain differences.

Front: 20 marker: Evaluate the view education serves capitalism
Back:
AO1: Marxist theory.
AO3: Evidence of inequality reproduction.
AO3: Functionalist critique.
Conclusion: Education supports capitalism but also serves other functions.
